coredata.dc:title: “All-in-one” carbon dots-based catalyst for converting CO2 to cyclic carbonates
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ruijia
Wan, Jianyong
Guo, Hongda
Tian, Bing
Li, Shujun
Li, Jian
Liu, Shouxin
James, Tony D.
Chen, Zhijun
coredata.dc:description: Converting CO2 with various epoxides to cyclic carbonates is extremely important. However, most of catalysts for such conversion either require complicated fabrication or are obtained from unsustainable sources. Motivated by this problem, we prepare biomass-based “all-in-one” carbon dots as nano-organocatalysts for firstly converting CO2 to cyclic carbonates. In this work, carbon dots (CD@KI) with high catalytic performance and good recyclability are prepared from sodium phytate (SP), polyethylenimine (PEI) and KI using one-pot hydrothermal treatment featuring cheap precursors and simple operation. Assisted by highly catalytic activity of CD@KI, up to 99.5% yield is achieved for converting CO2 and various epoxides to cyclic carbonates under mild conditions (solvent-free and 1 atm of CO2). Moreover, the conversion yield is maintained for reactions up to 10 g in scale. Significantly, by taking advantage of the inherent luminescence of CD@KI, the as-formed cyclic carbonates catalysed by CD@KI can be directly converted to afterglow polymer composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CO2 conversion
Luminescence
Polymer composites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Large-scale cycloaddition of CO2 to epoxides
CD@KI-catalysed cycloaddition of CO2 with epoxides
Scope of the CD@KI catalysed cycloaddition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Catalysts for synthesis of cyclic carbonate and CDs-catalysed organic reactions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM images of CD@KI, scale bar = 20 μm (up) and scale bar = 5 nm (down). (b) XRD pattern of CD@KI. (c) FT-IR spectra of SP, CD@non-KI and CD@KI. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CD@KI including high resolution on C1s scan (b), N1s scan (c), O1s scan (d), P2p scan (e) and I3d scan (f). (g) Thermal stability of CD@KI in CO2. (h) Absorbance (Abs) and fluorescence (FL) emission of CD@KI, excitation wavelength = 365 nm. (i) Lifetime of CD@KI, excitation wavelength = 365 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Recycling experiments for CD@KI-catalysed cycloaddition of CO2 to epoxide. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Application for PHU synthesis and its optical properties. (a) Synthesis of PHU from epoxides which was performed using CD@KI. (b) Photographs of CDs contained and removed PHU in bright field, upon UV excitation and switching off the UV sources. (c) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs remained PHU. (d) Phosphorescence lifetime of CDs remained PHU. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Plausible mechanism for CO2 cycloaddition with epoxide into cyclic carbonate catalysed by CD@KI.
Optimization of the reaction conditions a

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: 1 (5 mmol), CO2 (1 atm, balloon, ∼3 L), neat, 34 h.

head.ce:title: “All-in-one” carbon dots-based catalyst for converting CO2 to cyclic carbonates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Bath, Bath, BA2 7AY, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Converting CO2 with various epoxides to cyclic carbonates is extremely important. However, most of catalysts for such conversion either require complicated fabrication or are obtained from unsustainable sources. Motivated by this problem, we prepare biomass-based “all-in-one” carbon dots as nano-organocatalysts for firstly converting CO2 to cyclic carbonates. In this work, carbon dots (CD@KI) with high catalytic performance and good recyclability are prepared from sodium phytate (SP), polyethylenimine (PEI) and KI using one-pot hydrothermal treatment featuring cheap precursors and simple operation. Assisted by highly catalytic activity of CD@KI, up to 99.5% yield is achieved for converting CO2 and various epoxides to cyclic carbonates under mild conditions (solvent-free and 1 atm of CO2). Moreover, the conversion yield is maintained for reactions up to 10 g in scale. Significantly, by taking advantage of the inherent luminescence of CD@KI, the as-formed cyclic carbonates catalysed by CD@KI can be directly converted to afterglow polymer composites.

Carbon dots (CD@KI) with high catalytic performance were prepared from sodium phytate (SP), polyethylenimine (PEI) and KI using one-pot hydrothermal treatment, converting CO2 with various epoxides to cyclic carbonates under very mild reaction conditions. The work not only provides a convenient way for CO2 fixation, but also broads the application of CDs as catalysts using in organic reactions.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Ruijia Wang: Writing-Original Draft, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization, Data Curation.
Jianyong Wan: Data Curation, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation.
Hongda Guo: Data Curation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Validation.
Bing Tian: Methodology, Writing-Review & Editing, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Project administration.
Shujun Li: Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing.
Jian Li: Resources, Formal analysis, Validation, Supervision.
Shouxin Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing.
Tony. D. James: Methodology, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing.
Zhijun Chen: Methodology, Writing-Review & Editing, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Project administration.

The conversion of CO2 to high value-added chemicals is of vital importance in terms of CO2 neutralization and resource utilization as CO2 are abundant, non-toxic, inexpensive and renewable C1 feedstocks [1–6]. Among the widely reported conversion routes, the coupling reaction of CO2 with epoxides to synthesize cyclic carbonates features 100% atom-economy and sustainable process, has received significant attention from academics and industry as cyclic carbonates have wide applications in producing fine chemicals, polymers, fuels, pharmaceuticals and green electrolytes in batteries [7–15]. Thus, cost-effective and efficient catalytic transformation process is crucial for development of CO2 utilization.

Due to the relatively inert nature of CO2, this reaction requires a catalyst to proceed. As a result, numerous efficient catalysts have been developed including homogeneous catalysts and heterogeneous catalysts such as organo-catalyst [16–20], ionic liquids [21–25], metal complexes [26–29], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [30–33], covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) [34–36], functional polymers, etc. (Fig. 1 a) [37–39]. For these routes, tedious preparation procedure with multiple synthetic steps and specific conditions still restricted their further potential applications. Recently, carbon-based materials especially those graphene- and graphitic carbon nitride-based materials display as catalysts for CO2 fixation to cyclic carbonates [40–54]. Functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH– existed on the surface of carbon-based materials after modification could be used as efficient activation sites interacting with epoxides. Nevertheless, carefully modifications such as heteroatom-doping (B, N, S and P), metal-doping and co-catalyst anchoring, were still required to achieve highly catalytic activity [40–56]. Moreover, harsh conditions for synthesis and modification of catalysts, complex synthesis of co-catalysts, high reaction temperature and high CO2 pressure were still the problems for CO2 conversion. Therefore, the development of novel catalysts with ease preparation, sustainable precursors, high activity, mild conditions and good reusability is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs), firstly discovered in 2004 [57], are a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials (dimensions <10 nm), possessing many unique physiochemical and photochemical properties, making them promising materials for applications of imaging, environmental, catalytic, biological and energy-related field [58–62]. Abundant polar groups such as carboxyl acid, hydroxyl, carbonyl and amide on the surface of CDs could be used as acids, bases, and ligands which provide opportunities for organic catalysis and photocatalysis, possessing sustainable and green chemical processes [63,64]. For example, amino-rich CDs were used as efficient nano-organocatalysts for amino-catalysed conjugate additions and aldol reactions [65–67], condensations [68], reductive photocleavage reactions [69], and photocatalytic fluoroalkylation reactions [70]. CDs containing carboxylic acids or sulfonic acids groups on their surface can act as Brønsted acid catalysts in aza-Michael additions, condensation reactions [71], cross coupling reactions [72], and ring-opening reactions [73]. Aldol condensations smoothly achieved using hydroxyl-rich CDs as catalyst owing to its hydrogen bonds and photochemical properties (Fig. 1b) [74,75]. Inspired by these pioneering researches, we envision that these surface functional groups of CDs could be used as hydrogen-bond donators for activation of epoxides, providing potential for catalyzing the cycloaddition reaction between CO2 and epoxides.

In this study, we prepared an CDs-based nano-scale heterogeneous catalyst with biomass-derived sodium phytate (SP), readily available and inexpensive commercial material polyethylenimine (PEI), and co-catalyst KI through a one-pot hydrothermal method. Hydrogen-bonds for epoxide activation [76], nitrogen source from PEI for CO2 fixation, and co-catalytic effect of KI are assembled in one CDs catalyst, pointing to functions “all-in-one”. Such “all-in-one” CDs-catalysed cycloaddition of CO2 to epoxides features excellent catalytic activity and good functional group tolerance, which could transform epoxides to cyclic carbonates under mild reaction conditions of low CO2 pressure (1 atm), solvent-free process, and temperature of 80–100 °C (Fig. 1c). To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, CDs-catalysed CO2 cycloaddition with epoxides to cyclic carbonates is reported.

All chemicals were purchased from commercial sources and used without further purification, unless otherwise noted. Sodium phytate from rice was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. polyethylenimine (PEI, branched, average Mw∼800 by LS), potassium iodide (KI, AR, 99.0%), Chloroform-d, and dimethyl sulfoxide-d 6 were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All the epoxides were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). CO2 and N2 were supplied from Harbin Qinghua Gas Technology Co., Ltd with a purity of >99.5% and >99.999%, respectively.

Reactions were monitored by thin-layer chromatography (TLC) carried out on 25 mm silica gel plates. 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker-500 MHz spectrometer. The chemical shifts (δ) are given in parts per million relative to CDCl3 (7.26 ppm for 1H) or TMS (0 ppm for 1H). The chemical composition, content and state of the samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were acquired on a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometric (FT-IR) analysis of samples were recorded using a PerkinElmer Frontier FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Shimadzu XRD-6100 powder diffractometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a Netzsch STA 449 C instrument under a CO2 atmosphere. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) experiments were performed on a Waters 1515 in DMF. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were conducted using an Aprea 2S field emission scanning electron microscope (Thermo Fisher, USA). Flash column chromatography was performed on silica gel (particle size 230–400 mesh, purchased from Canada) and eluted with petroleum ether/ethyl acetate. The fluorescence spectra, afterglow spectra and lifetime decay curves of samples were recorded using a FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK), equipped with a xenon lamp and a 1-μs lamp (detector: photoelectric Multiplier, 200 nm < λ < 1700 nm). The afterglow emission spectra were recorded after a 10 ms delay.

The CDs catalysts were prepared by a simple hydrothermal treatment. In a typical synthesis, SP (0.341 g), PEI (0.133 g) and KI (0.6 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water (30 mL). The solution was heated to 180 °C for 8 h, and then allowed to cool to room temperature. The mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter after centrifugation. The solution was dialyzed against pure water through a dialysis membrane (cutoff Mn: 1.0 kDa) for 48 h. Then, the CD@KI was collected as a lightly yellow solid by freeze-drying (0.225 g, 21%wt yield).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Large-scale cycloaddition of CO2 to epoxides
ce:section.ce:para: 2-(Phenoxymethyl)oxirane 1a (15.017 g, 100 mmol), CD@KI (376 mg, 2.5 wt%) were weighted into a 250 mL flask, followed by charging with CO2. The reaction was stirred at 100 °C for 48 h under 1 atm of CO2 (bag, ∼35 L). After the reaction was completed, ethyl acetate (30 mL) was added and the mixture filtrated through a short pad of silica gel. The filtrate was concentrated under vacuum to give the desired product without further purification. 2a was obtained in 99.5% yield (19.3 g) with a high purity as detected by 1H NMR spectroscopy.

At the end of the reaction, 5 mL of cold n-hexane was added into the flask, the supernate was removed by a dropper after centrifugation. The solid residue was washed with cold n-hexane for two times followed by centrifugation and removal of solvent. The solid residue was dried in oven at 60 °C to remove the solvent in residue and reused for further consecutive runs.

Synthesis of poly(hydroxy urethane)s from epoxides Bisepoxide 1k (1.1112 g, 5 mmol) and 18.8 mg of CD@KI were weighted into a 25 mL flask, followed by charging with CO2. The reaction was stirred at 80 °C under 1 atm of CO2 (balloon) and monitored by TLC. After the reaction was completed, 20 mL DMSO and isophorondiamine (851.5 mg, 5 mmol, 1 equiv.) was added into the mixture and stirred at 100 °C for 10 h under N2. Upon completion, it was poured into 100 mL water and light brown precipitate was formed. The precipitate was filtered and dried to obtain poly(hydroxyurethane)s (CDs-remained PHU). For CDs-removed PHU synthesis, cyclic carbonate 2k was firstly purified by column chromatography on silica gel to remove the CD@KI, then used for polymerization with the same procedure as above.

The TEM was performed to investigate the morphologies and particle sizes of the synthesized carbon dots (Fig. 2 a). The TEM images indicated that all of the CD@KI are quasi-spherical and monodispersed, and most of them exhibits a diameter of 2–3.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM images reveal the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in CD@KI, attributed to the typical (100) lattice fringes of graphene. CD@KI was further evaluated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. CD@KI exhibits obvious KI diffraction peaks, which were in full agreement with KI standard PDF cards (Fig. 2b). The detailed chemical structure of CD@KI was subsequently characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). A broad signal centred at 3428 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of –OH groups and/or the presence of the N–H bonds. The peaks at 2988 and 2838 cm−1 correspond to C–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1780 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 belong to CO stretching vibration and N–H bending vibration, respectively. The peaks at 1460 cm−1 and 1356 cm−1 can be assigned to the aromatic C–N and C–O stretching, respectively. The peak at 1145 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of alkyl C–N bonds. XPS analysis indicated that the elemental fraction of CD@KI was C (55.4%), O (20.5%), N (10.5%), I (5.8%), Na (3.3%), P (3.0%), and K (1.5%) (Fig. 3 a).

The signals in high resolution spectra of C1s spectra at 284.8, 286.0 and 287.5 eV were attributed to -C–C-, -C–N- and –CO/-C–O-, respectively (Fig. 3b). The N1s component peaks at 399.2 and 401.4 eV were identified as -C–NH2 and -CO–NH-, respectively (Fig. 3c). -O–H, -PO and -C–O- bonds were detected at 530.8, 532.3 and 535.6 eV in the O1s spectra (Fig. 3d). The HPO4 2− and PO4 3− bonds were observed at around 134.2 and 133.3 eV for the CDs based on the high-resolution P spectra (Fig. 3e). All these results confirmed that CD@KI was rich in surface groups including –OH, -CO–NH-, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, and –NH2. These groups have been shown to exhibit great potential for the activation of the reaction between epoxides and CO2, which is particularly important for this application. In addition, TGA analysis was performed since the reaction between CO2 and epoxides is catalysed at high temperature. Giving that a practical reaction requires a CO2 environment, the TGA analysis of CD@KI was evaluated under a CO2 atmosphere. When the temperature was increased to 100 °C, the weight of the CD@KI did not exhibit any obvious change (Fig. 3g). When the temperature was further increased up to 200 °C, the weight of the CD@KI was reduced by just 2.2%. This result confirms that CD@KI displays nice thermal stability under a CO2 atmosphere, which is vital for facilitating the catalytic reaction. Besides these properties, CD@KI displays an absorbance centred in the UV region and exhibits fluorescence emission upon UV excitation (Fig. 3h). The quantum yield of CD@KI was 1.43% with a lifetime of 4.28 ns (Fig. 3i). All these results established that CD@KI possesses abundant surface groups, good thermal stability and fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD@KI-catalysed cycloaddition of CO2 with epoxides
ce:section.ce:para: CD@KI was then evaluated as a catalyst for the cycloaddition of CO2 with epoxides. We initially focused on the CD@KI (10 wt%) as the catalyst for the reaction of epoxide 1a with CO2 (1 atm, balloon) under solvent-free conditions at 80 °C for 34 h (Table 1 ). As expected, CD@KI exhibited excellent catalytic reactivity, affording cyclic carbonate 2a in a yield of 99% (entry 1). Decreasing the catalyst loading to 5 wt% also provided high levels of trans-formation with 99% yield in this cycloaddition process (entry 2). To our delight, the CDs catalyst still exhibited extraordinary catalytic reactivity as 2.5 wt% of the catalyst could afford 99% yield of corresponding product, which only 0.86 mol% of KI was involved with this catalyst according to the analysis of XPS in Fig. 3a (entry 3). However, when 10 mol% and 0.86 mol% of KI were used as sole catalyst in this reaction, a low yield (25%) or poor reactivity was observed when compared to CD@KI (entries 5 and 6), indicating that the activation of epoxides could be significantly enhanced by the CD@KI. Experiments using Na+-free CD@KI prepared by phytic acid (PA) instead of SP only afforded 68% yield for this reaction (entry 7), indicating that the Na+ in CDs is essential for the activation of epoxides as well as the hydrogen bonds. [77] In addition, control experiment using SP/KI as catalyst could improve the yield to 19% (entry 12) while only 9% yield was obtained using KI as catalyst (entry 5). PA was also used as co-catalyst for the same reaction, only 5% yield was obtained (entry 13) in the absence of Na+. This result indicates that Na+ could promote the reaction by effect of activation of epoxides. Notably, inert, or poor catalytic activity was observed for control experiments using CD@non-KI (carbon dots prepared without addition of KI), SP, and PEI as catalysts (entries 8, 10 and 11). Moreover, CD@non-KI with additional co-catalyst KI were used as catalyst for this reaction, showing lower catalytic activity than CD@KI (entry 9). The better catalytic performance of CD@KI probably attributed to a good dispersion of KI on CDs surface. Next, a synergic effect between SP, PEI, and KI was also investigated. An experiment using excess PEI (2.5 wt%), SP (2.5 wt%), and KI (0.86 mol%) as mixed catalyst was conducted, and only 43% yield was obtained, which was much lower than that of CD@KI (99% yield, entry 3). A weak synergic effect was indeed existed, resulting a light increased yield comparing to PEI (26% yield) or KI (9% yield) used as sole catalyst. This result indicates the superiority of functions “all-in-one” strategy. Compared to present carbon-based organocatalyst (such as graphene- and graphitic carbon nitride-based materials) catalysed cycloaddition of CO2 to epoxides, which usually required complex catalyst synthesis and harsh conditions for this transformation (high CO2 pressure and temperature), [9,10] this method exhibits advantages of green and mild operations in all processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scope of the CD@KI catalysed cycloaddition
ce:section.ce:para: With the optimized conditions, the scope of epoxides was further investigated for the cycloaddition reaction and the results are given in Table 2 . Substrates bearing a variety of functional groups could be successfully transformed into the desired products in excellent yields and selectivity in 34 h. Firstly, for sterically hindered epoxides containing aromatic rings and ether groups, the reaction proceeded smoothly and generated cyclic carbonates 2a-2d in satisfying yields (83%–99%) although the oxygen atom of ethers may interfere with the catalytic process through coordinating with the CDs catalyst via hydrogen bonds. In particular, for the challenging low reactivity substrate styrene oxide, CD@KI was also effective, at converting the epoxide to the corresponding cyclic carbonate 2e in 93% yield. In addition, simple aliphatic substrate hexyl epoxide afforded cyclic carbonate 2f in a yield of 96% under optimal conditions. A series of epoxides bearing synthetically useful functional groups, such as chloride (2g), bromide (2h), alkene (2i), and alkyl (2j), were suitable for the catalytic conditions and provided cycloaddition products in high yields (71%–95%). Moreover, the most challenging substrate bearing two epoxides was amenable to the current catalytic system, providing efficient access to bis(cyclic carbonate) 2k (86% yield), which could be used as an important precursor in the synthesis of polymer materials such as poly(hydroxy urethane)s (PHU) and polycarbonates.

Next, the large-scale experiment was investigated using this method. The reaction could be carried out on a 100 mmol scale to yield 19.3 g of cyclic carbonate 2a in a high yield of 99.5%. A high purity (>99%) of product can be obtained with a very simple purification process (filtrated through a short pad of silica gel and then concentrated under vacuum) (Scheme 1 ). This showcases the potential of the method's practical synthetic utility in their scalability for large-scale productions.

Recycling catalytic performance is an important indicator for assessment of heterogeneous catalyst. To explore the reusability of this “all-in-one” CDs-based catalyst, reaction of epoxide 1a with CO2 under standard conditions was carried out. For each run, the residue catalyst was collected by centrifugation and filtration. As shown in Fig. 4 , after 2 cycles of recycling, 98% yield of cyclic carbonate could be obtained, exhibiting highly catalytic activity. Although the catalytic activity of CD@KI reduced from the third run, a yield of 85% could also be achieved after five cycles, indicating a good reusability. The slight dissolution of CD@KI in epoxide 1a possibly lead to the loss of catalyst and further affect the recycling catalytic performance (Table S2).

The possible catalytic mechanism for the reaction between CO2 and epoxides to generate cyclic carbonates in the presence of CD@KI was shown in Scheme 2 . Firstly, the hydrogen bond between functional groups of hydroxyl, amine, and amide and the Lewis acidic alkali metal ion (K and I) in the CD@KI activate the epoxide O atom, leading to a ring-opening of the substrate by a nucleophilic attack of iodine anion. Subsequently, the CO2 inserts into the oxygen anion of the ring-opened epoxide to afford an alkyl carbonate anion intermediate. Finally, the intramolecular ring-closing leads to the formation of the cyclic carbonate product, regenerating the CD@KI catalyst. For alternative pathway, nitrogen-containing functional groups used for activation of epoxides and CO2 could also catalyze the cycloaddition reaction to yield cyclic carbonates. In this method, the functional groups as activation sites and the co-catalyst are assembled in one CDs, exhibiting excellent catalytic performance.

Considering of the good catalytic and fluorescence properties of CD@KI, the reaction was used to produce luminescent polymer composites. To this end, the synthesis of poly(hydroxy urethane)s was performed using the CD@KI-catalysed cycloaddition of CO2 with bisepoxide 1k and a sequential polymerization with isophorondiamine (IPDA) (Fig. 5 a) [78]. Using these conditions, poly(hydroxy urethane)s (PHU) were obtained with a number-average molecular weight (M
n ) value of 11944 g/mol and a dispersity of 1.66. In FT-IR spectra, the disappearance of the carbonyl stretching vibration of the carbonate at 1789 cm−1 and the appearance of the carbonyl stretching vibration band of the urethane at 1662 cm−1 indicate the successful formation of PHU. The as-obtained composite materials of CD@KI-contained PHU exhibited fluorescence emission, attributing to the optical properties of CD@KI (Fig. 5b–c). Moreover, afterglow emission with an ultralong lifetime of 1.96 ms (4.28 ns of lifetime for CD@KI powder) was observed in the composite film (Fig. 5d). This was probably attributed to the stabilization of triplet state of CD@KI by the rigid polymer matrix. Therefore, this process could be an enlightenment for organic synthesis of materials exhibiting room-temperature phosphorescence as the CDs show both phosphorescence and catalytic activity.

In summary, biomass-based carbon dots (CD@KI) are developed as robust nano-organocatalysts for firstly efficient conversion of CO2 with epoxides to cyclic carbonates. Highly catalytic activity and good recyclability are achieved by strategy of functions “all-in-one”. Various epoxides can smoothly be transformed into cyclic carbonates under green and mild conditions of solvent-free and 1 atm of CO2. This method offers easy access to various useful cyclic carbonates under mild and green reaction conditions, and also broads the application of CDs in the field of catalytically organic transformations. Notably, taking advantage of the inherent luminescence of CD@KI, the as-formed cyclic carbonates catalysed by CD@KI can be directly converted into afterglow polymer composites. Considering the great potential of luminescent polymer composites, our strategy could provide a powerful sustainable approach for producing luminescent devices using CO2.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31890774 and 32171716). Natural Science Funding of Heilong Jiang province for Excellent Young Scholar (YQ2020C017 and YQ2022C004) for financial support.
